text: The surgeon attempts circumferential dissection around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), aiming to harvest lymph nodes. Bleeding is observed from a lymph node during the skeletonization of the IMA, with visible fat pockets containing lymph nodes behind the artery.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and lighted catheters to assist in the dissection, aiming to elevate lymph nodes with the specimen. The surgeon navigates around the artery, employing electrocautery above and below the vessel, and proceeds with transection, resulting in visible bleeding.

The surgeon attempts to control bleeding using a vessel sealer, while the camera lens becomes smudged. Pressure is applied to the bleeding site, and a clip-applier is requested but not used. A 3-0 Prolene suture is then used to perform a figure-eight stitch, securing the area and controlling the bleeding.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the staple line, initially observing no bubbling. Upon closer inspection near the silk suture, bubbling is visible, indicating a potential leak.
The surgeon performs a second layer of reinforcement on the patient, placing layers of fat on top of each other. A leak test is conducted, showing no signs of leakage.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum while docked, using an Alexis wound protector to assist in the process. After closing the peritoneum, the surgeon undocks and proceeds to close the fan and seal incision.
